speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: On a local channel and I think they were associated with CNN. When I got back home. One of our customers where I was working in the pharmacy, said, I saw you on CNN.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made like a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were a little bit. So they talked to you about 10 minutes and you got about three seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a bite. They call it a bite.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bites, that's it. They are bites.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just slate it. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Third tape with Aldean Mason.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought we were just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aldean, when you got together on your 50th reunion, was there talk about the liberation of Buchenwald and taking care of all the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had-- we had our-- our book, and gave discussions of it, yes. And the fellows, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they remember it well-- working there and how horrible it was. And we didn't have-- I don't know how many doctors. We didn't have-- I think it was only two doctors that came, or three. But yes, we discussed that. We discussed our Cham, Germany. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you and Walt have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we do. We have one son, Kirk Mason, and he lives in Tampa, Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you told-- either one of you, or you told Kirk about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have. I started long ago, but he has been very interested in them in recent years. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and we have talked before, but he-- he's forgotten in the past, but now, he shows much interest in them. And I'm happy for that. And he's-- he has seen our pictures, and we've tried to give him the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that message?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that the younger generation should try and remember, learn from the experiences of the past, and-- and not let this happen again-- what happened before to them. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this here hatred that has gone through the years-- and [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think that's the main message. The-- the tolerance, they should learn the tolerance. And I think the more that is said with these videos that are coming out-- And when we went through, my husband and I, through the Shoah Foundation this week, they said that you're going to have a documentary in November, they're having one. And I just think this is marvelous-- and anything that's in the newspaper. And I had-- at the celebration-- there was a celebration Sunday night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here in Woodland Hills that I-- we both attended for Jack Pinto, the survivor. And I had sitting at the table, I had a lady come up to me, a teacher, and she said that she thought that it would be a good idea for-- to go-- she asked me if I had done any lecturing. And I said no, or to to on around. She said, I think this would be a good idea if you could go to some-- the schools or something and-- and talk to them about it, which I know is, and many people have. But I just thought that was interesting and I'll think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how you got in touch with Jack Pinto after all these years.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's unreal how I got a hold of Jack Pinto. I have here, this copy of And it is May 3rd, I believe, to the 5th. I don't see the writing on it. And it was in the Detroit Free Press, supplementary. And so I was-- this here picture just, oh-- really moved me after reading this and seeing these people and their faces.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I turned the page and started reading. And inside is-- I started reading about these survivors. And here, I saw this picture of Jack Pinto and read, 'and Jack Pinto.' And right away, I said to my husband, Jack Pinto, could this be Jack Pinto-- the one that I have a note-- had a note from, from Cham, Germany? I said, I am going to call the 800 number at the Shoah's-- the Survivors of the Shoah Monday, and I'm going to find out. I read the whole article. And I could just correlate with this article. I just thought the article was tremendous.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so on Monday, I called the 800 number and it was busy, and I tried again, it was busy. And I tried again, it was busy. So then, I went on with other duties and just let that pass. And I said, Tuesday, I am calling this 800 number and I did. And I had the sweetest person on the line, and her name was Ita. And so I explained to her that-- told her my name and explained to her that I had read the article and had a note from a Jack Pinto from a hospital in Cham, Germany. And she said, I will-- I will check that out, and she certainly did.

speaker: interviewee
text: She followed through, and she said, I will let you know what occurs on it, and she did. And when she called back, and told me that-- that it was the Jack Pinto, then she gave me permission to-- to call him. And so I said, I will call him that day, then. And I said, do you think it would be all right then to call him at 7:00 our Eastern Standard Time? And she said, yes. So I did. So it was very, very exciting. I can't believe that this could-- this is-- could be the Jack Pinto. I still can't believe it. This whole week has been unbelievable. What was your emotion?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he in the picture? Can you point to him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Point out Jack in the picture to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Jack Pinto is right here, and Dora is next-- his wife, next to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they are standing next to Steven Spielberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Steven Spielberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of this project that Mr. Spielberg has been promoting and producing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. I was aware of it. So when I read it, yes, I had read an article about him, from what he was doing on the East Coast. And so it wasn't a surprise. But this was a wonderful article, really explaining it much better-- it went into with these survivors, and the other article was just a news article. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then, of course, his picture in there. But I would have-- I would have read the whole article, whether the picture was there or not. And if they mention a name, I know that I would have probably-- I would have followed it through. So, yes, and I just think it's wonderful, what the Foundation is doing so far. And the word should spread further.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's drop it. I'm sorry. Can we drop the-- yeah, we don't need it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, up?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we don't need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can put the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction to Mr.-- your Mr. Pinto, Jack Pinto's reunion with you? I know it was a surprise. What was your feelings about seeing this gentleman after so many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantastic. After what he went through-- do you mean on the telephone or meeting, the reunion?

speaker: interviewer
text: Either one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the reunion, gala reunion-- really, they're fantastic. And like they had written up, the dance was like you know it was really happy, happy reunion. I still can't-- I cannot believe it. He is really fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were doing your 102nd-- the 120th Evacuation Hospital reunion, did you receive anything from-- from children of former members of the unit, that the children were now the only ones surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. We did. And yes, really, I mean, kind of tearful. I have one here. And I was amazed that they really cared, yes. And this one here, this is when we started the search. And this was in the beginning. And any time that I received any mail from any of them when we started this search, it really just-- I wanted to go on. Every day, I want to go on further then because it was just like a shot in the arm. But this one was received from a John O. Lafferty, Jr. From Lincolnton, North Carolina. And his father was First Lieutenant John, or John Lafferty, Jr., and I think he was promoted to Captain later on. But this was May 13, 1993, and it was addressed to Ruth Dixon-- Mrs. Ruth Dixon, in Spokane, Washington. And this John Lafferty then sent an enclosure to Walter and Aldean Mason. And it said, 'Dear Miss Dixon, I received a notice this past weekend addressed to Dr. John O. Lafferty with reference to the 50th anniversary reunion of the 120th Evacuation Hospital, which served in Europe during World War II. Dr. John O. Lafferty was my father. He died in December 1973.' And this is the part that I really loved. 'My father was profoundly moved by the Buchenwald Camp and believed strongly that the truth about this humanity should be widely published. He passed this belief on to my brother, my sister, and I.' And I just thought that was something, that John Lafferty did. And I-- I-- we were just pleased. We wrote back a letter to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe most members of your unit did pass that information on to their children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe that most of the members of your unit did pass that information on to their children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that maybe they have, and I believe that if they didn't in the beginning-- speak about it, like some of them, it's taken several years to talk about it. And they have in recent years, and perhaps because of the 50th anniversary of World War II, and coming up they've understood more. But there have been many-- now, when I say many, I know of some of the members of the unit have donated to Holocaust museums, like in New Jersey. And Lafferty, Dr. Lafferty, donated things to a Jewish Holocaust Museum in North Carolina, and one in Dallas, Texas, and in Kansas City, Missouri. And so I think the word is spreading, and I certainly hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: In spreading that word, you're given the opportunity-- we're giving you the opportunity to speak to future generations forever, coming down the line. What would you like to say then, as a witness to the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- just what I said, that-- that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they should remember the past and-- and it was real. And they should be aware of what happened, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shouldn't be-- what do I want to say? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They should be aware of the hatred that I mentioned, and not be subjected to it, with what is occurring now. And-- and we just hope for peace, and hope that we can all get along in this world. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it certainly happened. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say to those who say it didn't happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I-- I know it happened. I know it happened. I was a witness to it. It happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add that we haven't asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like to thank the Shoah Foundation for-- and-- and Mr. Spielberg for starting this. And I do hope the words will spread more, and-- and I'm sure it will and will continue to, and hope that maybe I could help to do a little something, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: We thank you, Aldean, for taking part in this. And we're going to bring on Jack Pinto in a few moments and share this moment with you on tape, and your husband, Walt. There is a saying from the Talmud, that when one saves a life, it is as if they have saved the entire world. You've done your duty and we thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce Jack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is Jack Pinto, a patient that was in the 120th Evacuation Hospital in Cham, Germany, at the Hotel Gress, where I was stationed. And I just had a wonderful reunion with Jack this week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, what would you like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: That the miracle is through that letter and through the photo what we had from Steve Spielberg, that she recognized me on that photo. She called up the Holocaust, the Shoah, and if I were Jack Pinto, who gave me-- gave me a letter? And they asked me. I said, I remember giving a letter 50 years ago, and I remember what I put it in there for thanking them, to help me save my life. And I hoped God will bring them back to their families and they came out. [CRYING] That was the letter what I wrote to her, and gave it to her. And that letter-- they found it. She still had it for 50 years ago. And we had a beautiful reunion. It was a big surprise for me, a very big surprise. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to say to Aldean besides thank you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she's a lovely woman. The way she saved that letter-- oh, that must have been such a lot of worth for her to do this. And I thank God and you to save that letter what I wrote. It's just a wonderful thing. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome, Jack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you first laid eyes on Aldean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I was in the hospital laying there and then she came on my bed. That's when I recognized her. I saw her many times. We talked together about what was going on, and what she went through when she went through Buchenwald, and all the bodies and everything. Because I know what it was, because I buried so many bodies, in [INAUDIBLE], that's in Silesia. But this must have been a terrible thing. And when she left, I gave her that letter. It was in my mind. I thanked her so much. And when she saved it all those years, it is a miracle. I thank God every night that we came together after 50 years. This was a big miracle and a blessing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to sing something to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I don't mind. I'll sing-- [SINGING] You must have been a beautiful baby. You must have been a beautiful child. When you were only starting to go to kindergarten, I bet you-- you drove the little boys wild. And when it came to winning blue ribbons, I could see the judges' eyes as they handed you the prize. You must have a beautiful baby, and look at you now.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're sweet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Jack, for participating in Aldean's testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once again, just grab his hand and then take it away. And then take it away.

speaker: interviewee
text: What does he--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and do it again. And take it away. And hold it again and just stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aldean, introduce your husband, Walt, to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd love to introduce my wonderful husband to you, Walt Mason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Walt, you wrote to Aldean after the war was over and you had been separated. What did you remember most about Aldean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thought she had a very sweet personality. She had a great profile. I wanted get to know her better, if that was possible at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been married how many years now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: 46 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you gotten to know each other very well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we thank you. Any words that you'd like to add to this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we want to thank the Foundation for the opportunity to do this, and the group that got Aldean and Jack together. I think it's been a tremendous spiritual and emotional roller coaster, you might say, but it's very satisfying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, maybe lean a little more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over here. That's good, that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, would you introduce your lovely wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Dora. And I met Dora in Amsterdam, in a hospital where I was laying. She was on the third floor. I was on the second floor. When she came from Sweden, where she was recuperating, she was nine months in Sweden. She could speak good Swedish, but she forgot it already. And when she came back, she came to the same place where I was in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years have you been married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September, it will be 48 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we thank you both for taking part.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a good woman, and the way she took care of me is unbelievable. Thank you, Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ready? Did you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Sophia Jorgenson Nelson. The picture was taken around 1912, in the state of Washington. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember where it was taken also?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Everett, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and when?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Am I to start? This is a picture of Aldean Nelson Mason, taken in 1941, as a student nurse in Everett, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] and Walt were talking. OK, I was talking to Jack, originally.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Lieutenant Aldean Nelson Mason, taken in 1944 in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the state of Washington. I'm not sure about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go. We are rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, do I say I'm-- oh, I'm-- I'm Aldean. Oh,

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of you as a nurse, looking out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Aldean Nelson Mason, the nurse in the middle, hanging out of the window in the apartment in Cham, Germany, looking at the enlisted men across the square. That's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably one of the enlisted men. You know, enlisted man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could it have been Walt?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could have been. Who knows? There's quite a few nurses there. And there were other nurses, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Walt Mason. The picture was taken in 1945, in Cham, Germany, at the 120th Evacuation Hospital that was billeted in Hotel Gress. This is Kirk C. Mason, the only son of Walter and Aldean Mason, taken in-- on Hutchinson Island, Florida, in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Kirk born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Kirk born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kirk was born 30th of May, 1957, and he is single.
